meta.description: Quote library for As It Fell — time-tagged verses from Sharp/Karpeles, English Folk Songs from the Southern Appalachians, revised and enlarged edition (Oxford University Press, 1932)
meta.session_notes: Session 1 (2026-03-23): Full triage complete. Vol. I (72 ballads) fully assessed; Vol. II (songs, hymns, nursery songs, jigs, play-party games) keyword-searched. All songs in both volumes assessed for time-of-day tags. 9 approved entries total. Songs already curated via campbell-sharp (1917 edition) were not duplicated. Key negatives confirmed: Lord Lovel, Barbara Allen, Daemon Lover, Trooper and the Maid, Lord Thomas, Fair Annie, Brown Girl, Green Bed, Three Butchers, Silk Merchant's Daughter, Edwin in the Lowlands Low, Pretty Nancy of Yarmouth — all assessed, no usable standalone time tags in Appalachian texts.
meta.notes: Quotes marked pending-wordsmith are proposed but not yet finalised. Do not push to app until status is changed to approved. Cross-check all candidates against as-it-fell-quotes-campbell-sharp.json before approving — the 1917 Campbell/Sharp edition is fully curated and many songs appear in both editions.

quotes.text:
As I walked out over London Bridge,
One misty morning early,
I overheard a fair, pretty maid
Was lamenting for her Geordie.
quotes.notes: Opening stanza. Morning tag explicit: 'one misty morning early'. The mist and bridge are atmospheric. Standard English broadside opening; confirmed in Sharp's Appalachian manuscripts (VWML CJS2/9/423 and CJS2/9/2542). Child 209.

quotes.text:
Early one morning before the break of day,
He went to his Polly, these words he did say:
"O Polly, O Polly, you must go with me,
Before we are married my friends for to see."
quotes.source: The Cruel Ship's Carpenter (Sharp/Karpeles 1932, Vol. I, No. 39)
quotes.notes: Opening stanza, standard Appalachian text. Morning marker explicit: 'before the break of day'. Pre-dawn approach; stands alone as image without murder context. Confirmed in multiple Sharp-collected versions including Sharp R (Clinton Fitzgerald, Royal Orchard, Afton, VA, April 28, 1918).

quotes.text:
Little Matty Groves he sleeps tonight,
Keeping your lady warm.
quotes.source: Little Musgrave and Lady Barnard (Sharp/Karpeles 1932, Vol. I, No. 23)
quotes.notes: The page's message to Lord Darnell. Night explicit; warmth in the dark. Two lines stand alone as image. Version A, sung by Mrs. Becky Griffin, Big Laurel, NC, Aug. 17, 1916. Not in as-it-fell-quotes-campbell-sharp.json — confirmed absent.

quotes.text:
Sweet William rose one morning bright
And dressed himself in blue.
quotes.source: Fair Margaret and Sweet William (Sharp/Karpeles 1932, Vol. I, No. 17)
quotes.notes: Opening lines. Morning explicit: 'one morning bright'. Some versions read 'one May morning' adding spring, but the Appalachian Stockton text has 'morning bright' without May. Collected from Jeff Stockton, Flag Pond, Unicoi Co., TN, Sept. 4, 1916.

quotes.text:
The day being gone and the night being come,
When most men were asleep,
Sweet William spied Lady Margaret's ghost
A-standing at his bed feet.
quotes.source: Fair Margaret and Sweet William (Sharp/Karpeles 1932, Vol. I, No. 17)
quotes.notes: Ghost-at-the-foot-of-the-bed stanza. Night and sleep both explicit. 'When most men were asleep' is the time marker; ghost appearance is the image. Mid-ballad. Appalachian text from Jeff Stockton / related versions in Sharp/Karpeles 1932.

quotes.text:
Every night when the sun goes in,
Every night when the sun goes in,
I hang down my head and mournful cry.
quotes.source: Every Night When the Sun Goes In (Sharp/Karpeles 1932, Vol. II)
quotes.notes: Opening verse. Evening marker explicit: 'when the sun goes in'. Triple repetition is structural to the song. Refrain ('True love, don't weep') omitted — first verse stands alone. Sung by Mrs. Wilson, Pineville, Bell County, KY, 1917. Text also reprinted in Lomax/Lomax, American Ballads and Folk Songs (1934), p. 149, which cites Sharp/Karpeles 1932 Vol. II as source.

quotes.text:
The chickens they are crowing, a-crowing, a-crowing,
The chickens they are crowing, for it is almost daylight.
quotes.source: The Chickens They Are Crowing (Sharp/Karpeles 1932, Vol. II)
quotes.notes: Opening verse, play-party section (new to 1932 edition; not in 1917 Campbell/Sharp). Dawn-threshold morning marker explicit: 'almost daylight'. Collected from Ben Finlay, Goose Creek (Little Goose Creek), Clay Co., KY, 1917.

quotes.text:
Bright morning stars are rising,
Bright morning stars are rising,
Bright morning stars are rising,
Day is a-breaking in my soul.
quotes.notes: Opening verse, hymns section (new to 1932 edition; not in 1917 Campbell/Sharp). Morning and pre-dawn markers explicit. Appalachian spiritual rather than British-derived ballad — distinct tradition within the collection.

quotes.text:
One morning in May, as I carelessly did stray,
I met a bold grenadier on my way.
quotes.source: One Morning in May (Sharp/Karpeles 1932, Vol. II)
quotes.notes: Opening two lines. Morning and spring both explicit. Collected in Kentucky. Cross-checked against as-it-fell-quotes-sharp.json — not a duplicate (Sharp 1916 has Down by the Green Bushes, a different song with a similar opening formula).
